q: A loan-approval pipeline must run three steps in a fixed order: fraud_check() → credit_pull() → record_decision(). The orchestrator prompt says 'Always call fraud_check before credit_pull, and never record a decision without both.' Audit logs show that in 0.4% of runs the model called credit_pull() first, and in 0.1% it recorded a decision after only fraud_check(). Compliance requires zero out-of-order executions. What is the correct fix?

options.A: Enforce the ordering in code with a deterministic state machine that tracks which steps have completed, exposes only the single valid next tool at each stage, and structurally refuses any out-of-sequence call by making the invalid tools unavailable until their prerequisites are satisfied
options.B: Strengthen the system prompt with capitalized ordering warnings and a few-shot example
options.C: Add an evaluator subagent that reviews each completed run, flags out-of-order executions, and routes the affected loans for manual reprocessing
options.D: Lower the sampling temperature to 0 so the model deterministically and repeatably follows the ordering instruction on every request

explanation: Compliance requires zero violations on a critical fixed sequence, which only deterministic/programmatic enforcement can guarantee. A is correct: a state machine or guarded wrapper makes out-of-order calls structurally impossible regardless of what the model chooses, because only the valid next tool is ever exposed and any other call is refused. B relies on probabilistic prompt compliance — the very thing already failing at 0.4%/0.1%; stronger wording and examples reduce but never eliminate the rate. C is reactive: it detects violations only after they happen and the decision was already recorded, and it adds manual toil rather than preventing the error. D is a misconception: temperature 0 makes sampling greedy/more repeatable but does not make instruction-following guaranteed, and the model can still deterministically choose a wrong order on some inputs.

q: An orchestrator dispatches one subagent per microservice to summarize that service's recent error logs. Each subagent returns its full raw log file (8k–20k tokens) plus a one-line summary. After 11 subagents the orchestrator's context is 94% full and it begins truncating earlier summaries before it can produce the cross-service report. What is the best design change?

options.A: Have each subagent return only its distilled conclusion — top error patterns, counts, suspected root cause — not the raw logs
options.B: Increase the orchestrator's maximum context window so that all 11 full raw log files plus their one-line summaries fit without any truncation during cross-service synthesis
options.C: Run the 11 subagents strictly sequentially so only one raw log file occupies the orchestrator's context at any given moment
options.D: Have the orchestrator collect all 11 raw logs first and then re-summarize each one itself before writing the cross-service report

explanation: Subagents exist partly to protect the orchestrator's context by returning distilled conclusions, not raw dumps. A is correct: compact synthesized findings let the orchestrator hold all 11 results and reason across them. B treats the symptom — a bigger window still fills with low-value raw text and costs more; the raw logs add no value at the orchestrator level. C does not help because the orchestrator must retain all summaries simultaneously to write a cross-service report; sequencing the workers does not shrink what must coexist at synthesis time. D is wasteful and self-defeating: it pulls every raw log into the orchestrator (the exact overflow) and duplicates work the subagents should have done.

q: A document-processing agent loops: it calls extract_page() then check_done(), repeating until check_done() returns true. On one malformed 3-page PDF, check_done() keeps returning false because page 3 never parses, and the loop has run 240 iterations re-extracting the same page, burning tokens and money. What is the most appropriate fix?

options.A: Add a programmatic guard that caps iterations and exits with a structured 'failed to converge' result when extraction stops progressing
options.B: Instruct the agent in its prompt to monitor its own behavior and stop as soon as it notices it is repeating the same extraction step
options.C: Replace the iterative loop with a single mega-prompt that asks the model to extract the full text content of every page in the malformed PDF in one combined pass
options.D: Add a second supervisor subagent whose sole job is to watch the first agent's iterations and instruct it to stop when stuck

explanation: Agent loops need a deterministic termination condition independent of model judgment. A is correct: a hard iteration cap plus a no-progress detector guarantees the loop ends and returns a structured failure the caller can handle. B relies on the model noticing its own repetition — unreliable, and it is precisely the kind of probabilistic self-monitoring that already failed implicitly. C changes the extraction strategy and may help throughput but does not provide a guaranteed stop condition, and a malformed page can still derail a single call without a bound. D adds a whole agent to do what one counter does, classic over-engineering, and the watcher itself relies on probabilistic judgment to decide when to intervene.

q: A pull-request reviewer is built as an orchestrator that spawns four subagents in parallel: a security scanner, a style checker, a test-coverage analyzer, and a final 'merge-decision' agent that needs all three prior results to decide. Currently all four launch at once and the merge agent frequently decides on incomplete data. How should the dispatch be structured?

options.A: Run security, style, and coverage in parallel, then add a barrier so the merge agent starts only after all three return
options.B: Run all four subagents strictly one after another in a fixed sequence to guarantee a predictable, well-defined execution ordering
options.C: Keep all four launching in parallel but have the merge agent poll the others' partial outputs and proceed once they appear complete
options.D: Merge the four roles into one agent that performs security, style, coverage, and the final decision sequentially in a single shared context

explanation: The first three tasks are independent and the fourth has a true data dependency on all of them — the textbook case for parallel fan-out plus a barrier. A is correct: it maximizes concurrency for the independent work while ensuring the dependent step waits for complete inputs. B is needlessly slow: it serializes three tasks that have no dependency on each other, wasting the parallelism. C reintroduces the original bug — guessing completion is non-deterministic and is what caused decisions on incomplete data. D throws away the isolation and parallelism benefits and crams unrelated concerns into one context, increasing error and token pressure without solving the dependency-ordering issue cleanly.

q: A user asks an agent, 'What's the current value of the TIMEOUT_MS constant in config/app.yaml?' The team's framework is configured to spin up a coordinator that decomposes every request into planning, research, and synthesis subagents. For this request the overhead adds ~30s and three extra model calls. What is the right architectural judgment?

options.A: Handle it directly by reading the one named file and returning the value; a single-fact lookup does not justify multi-agent orchestration
options.B: Keep the planning, research, and synthesis decomposition so that every request follows the same uniform, predictable processing pipeline
options.C: Add a fourth caching subagent that stores resolved constant values so repeated lookups of the same config key return faster next time
options.D: Spawn one subagent per config file in the repository to thoroughly search every location where the requested constant might possibly be defined

explanation: Multi-agent orchestration earns its overhead only when a task has genuine breadth, parallelism, or context-isolation needs. A is correct: a single known file and a single fact is trivially handled inline; the orchestration is pure overhead. B values uniformity over fitness — forcing every trivial query through a heavy pipeline wastes latency and tokens. C invents complexity (a caching agent) to speed up a process that should not exist for this task. D is over-decomposition: fanning out across all config files to find a value the user already located in one named file multiplies cost for no benefit.

q: A data-migration subagent is given the full toolset of the parent: read_table, write_table, drop_table, send_email, and deploy_service. Its only job is to read rows from a staging table and return row counts per status. A post-incident review finds the subagent once called drop_table on a misread instruction. What is the least-privilege fix?

options.A: Grant the subagent only read_table plus whatever strictly read-only tools its counting task actually requires, and withhold write_table, drop_table, send_email, and deploy_service entirely so that the destructive and side-effecting tools are not even present in the toolset it can ever invoke
options.B: Keep the full toolset available but add a system-prompt instruction telling the subagent never to call drop_table
options.C: Wrap drop_table in a confirmation guard that requires the subagent to explicitly confirm twice before the destructive deletion is actually executed
options.D: Give the subagent read_table and write_table for its counting work but remove only the destructive drop_table tool from its available set

explanation: Least privilege means a subagent receives only the tools its task requires. A is correct: a read-and-count task needs read access only, so write/drop/email/deploy should not even be in its toolset — a tool it cannot see cannot be misused. B relies on probabilistic prompt compliance to avoid a destructive action; the incident shows that is not safe for irreversible operations. C still exposes a destructive tool and leans on the model to refuse — double-confirm prompts are model-driven and bypassable. D removes only one dangerous tool while leaving write_table and the email/deploy capabilities exposed, which still violates least privilege for a read-only task.

q: An orchestrator fans out five subagents to gather data for a quarterly report. Subagent 3 (regional sales for APAC) times out after 60s having retrieved 2 of 4 countries. It returns nothing, and the orchestrator silently proceeds, producing a report that omits APAC entirely with no warning. What is the best handling pattern?

options.A: On timeout, have the subagent return a structured partial result: the 2 countries it got plus a flag listing the 2 missing
options.B: On timeout, have the subagent return a generic 'error' string so the orchestrator at least knows that something went wrong during retrieval
options.C: Have the orchestrator treat any non-response as a success and continue, on the assumption that most of the required data is usually present anyway
options.D: Increase every subagent's timeout to 10 minutes so that slow data sources finish in time and timeouts effectively never occur in practice

explanation: Partial results plus structured context let the coordinator recover intelligently. A is correct: returning the data it did obtain along with an explicit list of what is missing lets the orchestrator retry just the gap, annotate the report, or degrade transparently. B is too coarse — a generic error tells the orchestrator nothing about what was retrieved or what to retry, so the recoverable partial data is lost. C is the actual bug: silent success on missing data is how APAC vanished without warning. D pretends the failure mode can be configured away; long timeouts increase latency for everyone and still do not handle the case where a source is genuinely unavailable.

q: An evaluator-optimizer loop refines a generated SQL query: the optimizer rewrites the query, the evaluator scores it on correctness and cost. After 30 rounds the score plateaus at 0.82 and the two agents keep exchanging near-identical versions, never reaching the 0.95 target. Logs show the evaluator's feedback has become vague ('could be more efficient') with no actionable specifics. What is the best intervention?

options.A: Add a stopping criterion (max rounds plus a minimum-improvement threshold), require specific actionable critique, and return the best candidate on a stall
options.B: Remove the stopping logic entirely and let the optimizer-evaluator loop keep iterating on the same SQL query until it finally reaches the exact 0.95 correctness-and-cost target, no matter how many rounds that takes
options.C: Replace the separate evaluator by having the optimizer score and judge its own rewritten output each round, saving one model call per iteration
options.D: Raise the target score from 0.95 to 0.99 to push both the optimizer and the evaluator to work harder and produce a more refined query

explanation: Evaluator-optimizer loops need bounded iteration and concrete feedback to converge or exit gracefully. A is correct: a minimum-improvement threshold breaks the plateau, actionable criteria give the optimizer something to act on, and returning the best-so-far prevents infinite spinning. B guarantees a possible infinite/expensive loop when the target may be unreachable for this query. C removes the independent check that makes the loop work — self-evaluation tends to be over-generous and would lose the separation that drives improvement. D raises the bar the loop already cannot clear, worsening the non-termination rather than fixing the vague-feedback root cause.

q: A research orchestrator decomposes 'analyze our top 3 competitors' pricing, hiring, and product velocity' into subagents. The current decomposition creates one subagent per competitor, and each is told to cover all three dimensions. Results are inconsistent: one subagent deeply covered pricing but skipped hiring, another did the reverse. The synthesis step has lopsided coverage. How should the decomposition be improved?

options.A: Give each subagent an explicit bounded scope with a checklist of the exact dimensions and output fields it must return, so coverage is uniform
options.B: Add a fourth 'coverage' subagent that reads the three competitor reports and fills in any missing dimensions from its own prior knowledge
options.C: Collapse to a single subagent that handles all three competitors across all three dimensions in one pass to keep coverage perfectly consistent
options.D: Spawn nine subagents, one per competitor-dimension cell, so each narrow slice is researched independently regardless of cost or overlap between them

explanation: Task-decomposition quality depends on giving each worker a clear, bounded scope and a defined output contract. A is correct: an explicit checklist of dimensions and required fields produces uniform, mergeable results and eliminates the lopsided coverage. B papers over the gap by inventing missing analysis from a model's prior knowledge rather than actual research — unreliable and possibly fabricated. C removes parallelism and overloads one context with nine cells, the original under-specification problem at larger scale. D is over-engineering on the other extreme: nine subagents may be defensible only if each cell is genuinely heavy, but here it multiplies orchestration cost and synthesis complexity when a clear per-competitor contract already fixes the issue.

q: An order-fulfillment agent must, for refunds over $500, always call manager_approval() before issue_refund(). Product wants this guaranteed even as prompts evolve across releases. A proposal suggests adding a clear instruction and a few-shot example to the system prompt. Over a 50k-request canary, 7 refunds over $500 were issued without approval. What should ship to production?

options.A: A programmatic guard inside issue_refund() itself that refuses to process any refund above $500 unless a valid, unexpired approval token issued by manager_approval() is present and verified, making the unapproved over-$500 path structurally impossible regardless of how the surrounding prompts drift across future releases
options.B: A more emphatic system prompt stating the approval rule plus several few-shot examples of the over-$500 flow
options.C: A nightly evaluator subagent that audits the day's refunds and emails the finance team about any over-$500 refund that was issued without approval
options.D: Routing every refund request through a dedicated refund subagent that carries the same approval instruction and few-shot example in its own system prompt

explanation: A hard business rule on a money-moving action must be enforced deterministically, not by prompt compliance. A is correct: making issue_refund() itself require a valid approval token closes the gap regardless of prompt drift — the unsafe path becomes impossible. B is the approach that already let 7 violations through; more emphasis lowers but never zeroes a probabilistic rate. C catches violations after the money has left, which is too late for a control that must prevent the action. D just relocates the same fallible prompt instruction into a subagent; the enforcement layer is still probabilistic and will still leak.

q: A coordinator launches a code-search subagent and a dependency-graph subagent in parallel. The code-search subagent crashes with a tool exception mid-run. It returns the literal Python traceback string to the coordinator, which inserts the traceback into its next prompt and then hallucinates a 'fixed' file path that doesn't exist. What is the better error-propagation design?

options.A: Have the subagent catch the failure and return a structured error object: the operation, the failed tool, work completed, and whether retry is safe
options.B: Have the subagent return the raw Python traceback verbatim to the coordinator, on the principle that maximum diagnostic detail gives it the most to work with
options.C: Have the subagent swallow the exception silently and return an empty success result so the parallel pipeline keeps moving without stalling on the failure
options.D: Have the coordinator immediately abort the entire run, including the parallel dependency-graph subagent, the moment any subagent throws an unhandled exception

explanation: Coordinators recover best from structured error context, not raw stack traces or silence. A is correct: a typed error describing the failed operation, partial progress, and retry-safety lets the coordinator retry, route around, or report cleanly. B dumps an unstructured traceback into the prompt, which is what led the coordinator to hallucinate — raw traces are noisy context, not actionable signal. C is silent-success, hiding the failure and corrupting downstream results with a fake empty answer. D over-reacts: aborting the whole run on any single failure throws away the parallel dependency-graph work that may have succeeded and prevents graceful degradation.

q: A team is deciding whether to convert a single-agent invoice processor into a multi-agent system. The task is: read one PDF invoice, extract 6 fields, and write them to a database — done thousands of times a day, each invoice independent and self-contained. A junior engineer proposes a coordinator with extractor, validator, and writer subagents per invoice. What is the soundest recommendation?

options.A: Keep it as a single agent, or an equivalent deterministic per-invoice pipeline, and scale horizontally by running many independent instances; the narrow, self-contained extract-six-fields-and-write task gains nothing from multi-agent coordination and would only pay extra latency and token cost for the orchestration
options.B: Adopt the three-subagent extractor-validator-writer design, since separating each concern reliably improves correctness
options.C: Adopt the three-subagent design and also parallelize across invoices, with one coordinator managing all in-flight invoices together in a shared context
options.D: Run an evaluator-optimizer loop on every invoice that iteratively refines the six extracted fields until the extraction reaches near-perfect accuracy

explanation: Multi-agent overhead is justified by breadth, heavy parallel context needs, or hard isolation requirements — none present here. A is correct: a narrow, self-contained per-invoice task is best served by one agent or a plain deterministic pipeline; you can still scale by running many independent instances. B treats 'separation of concerns' as a universal good, but for a trivial extraction it just multiplies model calls and coordination latency. C compounds the over-engineering and adds a shared-context coordinator that would bloat context across thousands of invoices. D applies an iterative refinement loop to a deterministic extraction problem, adding rounds of cost for a task that does not need open-ended optimization.

q: An orchestrator collects outputs from three subagents that researched a vendor: one returns a glowing security assessment, one returns a critical one citing a recent breach, and one returns 'insufficient data.' The current synthesis step naively concatenates all three into the final answer, producing a self-contradictory report. What is the best synthesis approach?

options.A: Have synthesis reconcile the findings: weigh the evidence, surface and explain the disagreement, and produce one coherent assessment noting the data gap
options.B: Have synthesis select whichever subagent produced the most text for its assessment, since greater length is a reasonable proxy for analytical thoroughness
options.C: Have synthesis average the three assessments into a single neutral verdict so the final report avoids taking sides on the conflicting security findings
options.D: Re-run all three subagents repeatedly until their assessments converge on agreement, then concatenate the now-consistent outputs into the final report

explanation: Synthesis means reconciling and integrating worker outputs into a coherent whole, especially when they conflict. A is correct: a good synthesizer weighs the evidence (a cited recent breach is material), explicitly surfaces the disagreement, and flags the data gap rather than hiding it. B uses length as a proxy for quality, which is unfounded and could elevate the wrong conclusion. C blends contradictory signals into a mushy 'neutral' verdict that buries a real security concern. D assumes consensus is achievable and forces re-runs until agreement, which can loop indefinitely and pressures workers toward false agreement instead of honest synthesis of genuine conflict.

q: A deployment agent's workflow must run run_tests() then, only if tests pass, call deploy(). The team implemented this purely via the prompt: 'Run the tests; if they pass, deploy.' Metrics show that under long, busy conversations the model occasionally deploys after a partial or failed test run because it misreads the test output. Deploys to production are irreversible within the window. What is the correct enforcement?

options.A: Gate deploy() on a machine-checked test artifact such as an exit code or signed pass token; deploy() refuses to run unless that artifact says pass
options.B: Move the conditional deploy instruction to the very top of the system prompt and repeat it again at the end so the model is far more likely to honor it
options.C: Add a reviewer subagent that reads the full conversation after each deploy attempt and decides whether the deployment looked justified given the test output
options.D: Ask the model to explicitly restate the run_tests() results in its own words immediately before deploying so it reads the test output more carefully

explanation: An irreversible production action conditioned on a pass/fail fact must be enforced by checking a machine-verifiable artifact, not by the model's reading of text. A is correct: gating deploy() on an exit code or signed token removes the model's interpretation from the critical path — it physically cannot deploy on a failed/partial run. B and D both rely on the model correctly interpreting test output, the exact failure observed under long contexts; placement and restatement reduce but do not eliminate misreads. C is reactive and itself probabilistic — the reviewer subagent can also misjudge, and by the time it reviews, the irreversible deploy may already be done.

q: An orchestrator dispatches eight independent enrichment subagents (each annotating a different shard of a dataset) and waits for all eight before writing output. Today it waits for them strictly one after another even though they share no data, and end-to-end latency is ~8x a single shard. There is a barrier only at the final write. How should dispatch change?

options.A: Dispatch all eight concurrently since the shards are independent, then barrier once before the final write, cutting wall-clock time toward the slowest shard
options.B: Keep the eight subagents running strictly one after another, because launching them concurrently risks subtle race conditions and data corruption even when the shards share no underlying state
options.C: Reduce to a single subagent that processes all eight shards itself in one context, avoiding any cross-subagent coordination or dispatch overhead entirely
options.D: Introduce a coordinator-of-coordinators layer that groups the eight subagents into two tiers of four to manage the fan-out in a more structured way

explanation: Independent tasks should run concurrently; a barrier is only needed before a step that requires all prior results. A is correct: the shards share no data, so fanning out all eight in parallel and barriering only at the write collapses latency toward the slowest shard. B invents a race condition where none exists — independent shards with no shared state have nothing to race on. C eliminates parallelism entirely, serializing all eight into one agent and making latency worse, not better. D adds a pointless extra orchestration tier; tiering four-and-four provides no benefit for eight already-independent tasks and only adds coordination overhead.

q: A customer-support orchestrator routes a ticket to a billing subagent and a logistics subagent in parallel. The billing subagent finishes and returns a confident answer, but it depended on the logistics subagent's shipment status, which had actually failed and returned a default placeholder of 'shipped.' The orchestrator merged both and told the customer their refund was processed based on a delivery that never happened. What design flaw should be fixed first?

options.A: Eliminate the silent default entirely: when the logistics lookup fails it must return an explicit failure or 'unknown' status rather than a placeholder of 'shipped', and the billing subagent must be designed to withhold the refund and not proceed whenever the shipment status it depends on is unconfirmed or unavailable
options.B: Add billing subagents that independently recompute and cross-check the refund amount before merging
options.C: Increase the orchestrator's sampling temperature so it explores more possible interpretations of the subagent outputs before it merges them into a reply
options.D: Make the billing and logistics subagents run sequentially so that billing always executes after logistics has finished returning its shipment status

explanation: The root cause is a silent default masquerading as real data, which corrupted the merge. A is correct: error context must be truthful — an explicit failure/unknown status lets the orchestrator and billing logic withhold the refund until shipment is confirmed, instead of acting on a fabricated value. B adds redundancy to the wrong layer; the refund amount was never the problem, the false shipment status was. C misuses temperature, which controls sampling randomness and would make merging less predictable, not more correct. D fixes ordering but not the core defect: even running last, billing would still consume the fabricated 'shipped' default and make the same error — the placeholder, not the sequencing, is the bug.
